Multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation (MRAG)

Goal of this Module

The goal is to demystify Multimodal RAG (MRAG), the advanced evolution of classic text RAG that handles queries and knowledge across text, images, tables, documents, video, and audio. You'll learn:

  • Why MRAG is harder but more powerful than text RAG
  • Core pipeline stages: query understanding ΓåÆ retrieval ΓåÆ reranking ΓåÆ context packing ΓåÆ grounded generation
  • Three main retrieval paradigms (text-first, vision-first, joint) and their trade-offs
  • Practical techniques for document/table handling, visual grounding, cross-modal reranking, and iterative/multi-hop retrieval
  • How to avoid common pitfalls like hallucinations, modality bias, and context explosion This equips you to build production MRAG systems (e.g., TechVista support bot) that truly "see" customer photos, read spec sheets, and give accurate, cited answers.

Text RAG is simple: chunk text ΓåÆ embed ΓåÆ retrieve similar chunks ΓåÆ stuff into LLM prompt ΓåÆ generate.

MRAG introduces complexity at every stage because information lives in images, tables, videos, audio, and their combinations. Key differences:

  • Input queries can be multimodal (text + photo of broken phone).
  • Retrieval targets include non-text (raw images, doc regions, video frames + transcripts).
  • Embeddings need cross-modal alignment (text "wireless ANC earbuds" should match product photos + spec tables).
  • Context is richer but messier, images/tables can't be naively concatenated like text.
  • Generation requires multimodal models (VLMs like GPT-4o, Gemini, LLaVA-Next) to reason over mixed inputs and output grounded text (sometimes with images/tables inline).
  • Pitfalls multiply: visual hallucinations, modality bias (trusting text over image), poor cross-modal retrieval, context packing explosions.

Surveys emphasize: MRAG reduces hallucinations by grounding in visual evidence, but only if retrieval preserves semantics and generation stays faithful to retrieved multimodal content.

MRAG Core Components

Modern MRAG pipelines follow this flow

Multimodal Query Understanding

  1. The system first parses what the user actually wants ΓÇö and in which modalities.
    • If query is pure text ΓåÆ standard embedding.
    • If query includes image (e.g., customer uploads cracked screen photo) ΓåÆ use VLM to summarize or extract intent ("user is showing damaged earbuds, likely warranty query").
    • Hybrid: fuse text + image embeddings for the query vector.
  2. Practical at TechVista: Customer types "This isn't working" + uploads photo ΓåÆ VLM understands "query about faulty wireless earbuds + visual evidence of defect" ΓåÆ creates a rich query embedding that searches both text specs and visual damage patterns.

Multimodal Retrieval

  1. The heart of MRAG, fetch relevant chunks from the corpus.
    • Use hybrid index (from Module 4): dense vectors (CLIP/SigLIP for images, ColPali for doc pages) + sparse (BM25 for text).
    • Retrieve across modalities: text chunks, image-text pairs, full doc regions (with layout), video clips + ASR transcripts.
    • Often multi-vector: one vector per modality, late fusion at retrieval time.
  2. Example: For "Compare ANC on Sony WH-1000XM5 vs Bose QuietComfort" ΓåÆ retrieve product photos, comparison tables from PDFs, unboxing video snippets, review audio clips.

Context Selection / Reranking

  1. Raw retrieval often returns noise, rerank to pick the most useful pieces.
    • Use cross-modal reranker (e.g., fine-tuned ColBERT-style or VLM-based scorer).
    • Filter by metadata (reliability ΓëÑ 8, recent timestamps).
    • Diversity reranking to avoid redundant images.
    • Limit to top-5ΓÇô10 to fit context window.
  2. At TechVista: Initial retrieval might grab 20 items ΓåÆ reranker scores them on relevance to the damage photo + query ΓåÆ keeps the clearest crack images + accurate spec tables.

Multimodal Generation (Grounded Responses)

  1. Feed selected multimodal context to a VLM ΓåÆ generate answer that cites visuals.
    • Prompt engineering: "Use the provided images/tables only ΓÇö describe exactly what you see."
    • Output format: text explanation + inline references ("As shown in Image 2, the crack is on the right side...").
    • Advanced: agentic loop (if unclear, retrieve more or ask clarifying question).
  2. Result for TechVista customer: "Your earbuds show physical damage on the charging port (see retrieved photo). Warranty covers manufacturing defects but not accidental damage ΓÇö check policy page 3 table."

Retrieval Targets: What You Actually Fetch

Surveys classify targets by granularity and modality:

  • Text only ΓåÆ fast fallback, but loses visual nuance (e.g., misses chart colors).
  • Image-text pairs ΓåÆ CLIP-style (product photo + caption/description), great for visual search.
  • Document regions ΓåÆ page/figure/table bounding boxes (ColPali embeds layout-aware), ideal for manuals/spec sheets.
  • Video clips + transcripts ΓåÆ keyframes + ASR text, for unboxing/review videos (e.g., "Show me how to pair these headphones").

TechVista example: Query "Why does my smartwatch screen scratch easily?" ΓåÆ retrieve product close-up photos, durability test video clip (with subtitles), scratched-unit customer images, spec sheet region on "Gorilla Glass".

Context Packing: How to Format Mixed Content Without Losing Grounding

This is a huge practical pain point, VLMs have token limits, and naive packing destroys meaning.

Common strategies from recent works:

  • Image placeholders + descriptions Prompt: "Image 1: [description from caption or short VLM summary]. Image 2: ..." Then reference "Look at Image 1's crack pattern".
  • Multimodal prompt templates Use special tokens like <image> or base64-encoded images (if model supports). Order matters: put most relevant images first.
  • For tables Convert to markdown + embed image of table ΓåÆ "Table 1 (image attached): Battery life comparison..."
  • Compression tricks Summarize long videos to key frames + highlights. Use token-efficient packing (group related chunks).
  • Grounding enforcement Add instructions: "Only use information visible in the retrieved images/tables. If uncertain, say so." Post-generation check: verify claims against retrieved regions.

Pitfall example: Packing 10 images without order ΓåÆ model ignores half.

Solution: Reranker sorts by relevance score + diversity ΓåÆ pack top 4ΓÇô6 with clear labels.

Retrieval in MRAG: Three Main Paradigm

Retrieval in MRAG isn't just ΓÇ£find similar stuff.ΓÇ¥ Because information lives in different forms (text descriptions, product photos, comparison tables, blurry customer-uploaded defect images, 30-second unboxing clips), you need different ways to hunt for relevance. The field has converged on three main paradigms

Text-first Retrieval (OCR / Captions ΓåÆ Retrieve)

Convert everything possible to text first, then use a powerful text retriever (BM25, dense text embeddings like e5-large, or even late-stage LLM reranking). Visual information is ΓÇ£squeezedΓÇ¥ into words before retrieval happens.

How it works in depth:

  • For born-digital PDFs (clean manuals from Sony/Bose): extract text directly with PyMuPDF or pdfplumber.
  • For scanned PDFs or product images: run heavy-duty OCR (TrOCR, Donut, Nougat, or layout-aware models like LayoutLMv3) ΓåÆ get raw text + sometimes layout hints.
  • For tables: convert to markdown/CSV via Table Transformer or deepdoctection ΓåÆ flatten into text like ΓÇ£Model | Battery Life | ANC Level\nWH-1000XM5 | 30h | YesΓÇ¥.
  • For images/videos: generate captions (BLIP-2, LLaVA captioning, or even short VLM summaries like ΓÇ£close-up of cracked phone screen near charging portΓÇ¥) and store those captions + OCR-extracted text.
  • Then embed the text (or chunks) with a text encoder ΓåÆ retrieve with cosine similarity or hybrid sparse+dense.

Advantages (why companies still love it in production):

  • Text retrievers are mature, fast, cheap (BM25 is lightning), and interpretable.
  • You can do keyword filtering (ΓÇ£ANCΓÇ¥ + ΓÇ£SonyΓÇ¥) very reliably.
  • Works even when vision models are expensive or slow.

Big downsides (the reason it's no longer the only game):

  • Massive information loss: layout, colors, spatial relationships, fine visual details vanish.
  • OCR errors compound (misread serial number ΓåÆ wrong warranty policy retrieved).
  • Captioning is lossy: a blurry damage photo might get captioned ΓÇ£damaged deviceΓÇ¥ ΓåÆ useless for distinguishing crack vs scratch vs dent.

Customer uploads photo of earbuds case with visible crack + types ΓÇ£Is this covered?ΓÇ¥.

Text-first system: OCR/caption ΓåÆ ΓÇ£wireless earbuds case with damageΓÇ¥ ΓåÆ retrieves generic warranty text saying ΓÇ£physical damage not coveredΓÇ¥ ΓåÆ answer is correct but vague (doesn't point to exact photo evidence).

Result: Customer feels ΓÇ£the AI didn't really look at my picture.ΓÇ¥

Vision-first Retrieval

Treat the visual modality as primary. Embed raw images/pages/frames directly with a vision or vision-language model ΓåÆ retrieve based on visual similarity, ignoring or minimally using text.

How it works in depth:

  • Use models like CLIP, SigLIP, ImageBind, or newer ones like ColPali (which embeds document pages as images without OCR).
  • For documents: render PDF page as high-res image ΓåÆ feed to ColPali/PaliGemma ΓåÆ get multi-vector embeddings per patch/token.
  • For product photos/customer uploads: direct CLIP embedding of the raw JPEG.
  • For video: extract keyframes every 2ΓÇô5s ΓåÆ embed each ΓåÆ aggregate or retrieve top keyframes.
  • Retrieval is pure cosine similarity in vision embedding space.

Advantages:

  • Preserves visual nuance perfectly, color gradients, texture of crack, layout of table columns.
  • No OCR errors, ColPali famously beats OCR-based methods on document retrieval benchmarks because it ΓÇ£seesΓÇ¥ the page like a human.
  • Great for visual similarity search (e.g., ΓÇ£find products that look like this damaged oneΓÇ¥).

Downsides:

  • Text semantics can be weak, ΓÇ£ANCΓÇ¥ keyword won't match unless visually associated.
  • Compute-heavy at scale (embedding every PDF page as image is expensive).
  • Hard to do keyword filtering or exact match.

Query: ΓÇ£Show me phones with similar scratch pattern to this uploaded photo.ΓÇ¥

Vision-first: embed uploaded scratch photo with CLIP ΓåÆ retrieve visually similar product damage images from catalog + customer returns ΓåÆ perfect matches even if captions are generic.

Joint Retrieval

Don't choose one modality, use both (or all) in a coordinated way

Paired / Aligned Embeddings: - Pre-align text and image in shared space (CLIP-style) so ΓÇ£ANC earbudsΓÇ¥ text embedding is close to photos of earbuds with ANC icon. Retrieve with joint similarity (weighted sum or late fusion: s = ╬╗┬╖s_text + (1-╬╗)┬╖s_image).

Cross-Encoders / Cross-Modal Rerankers: - First-stage bi-encoder retrieval (fast, cheap) gets top-100 candidates ΓåÆ second-stage cross-encoder (slow, accurate) scores query+document pair jointly (takes image patches + text tokens together). Models like MM-R5 or multimodal ColBERT do this.

Answer-aware Reranking: - Use the candidate context to generate a provisional answer ΓåÆ rerank based on how well the candidate supports that provisional answer (self-consistency or answer-relevance scoring).

Why it's powerful:

  • Combines speed of first-stage + precision of second-stage.
  • Handles modality imbalance (trust image more when text is noisy).
  • ΓÇ£Answer-awareΓÇ¥ variant dramatically reduces hallucinations,if provisional answer says ΓÇ£crack on left sideΓÇ¥ but candidate image shows right side, downrank it.

Complex query: ΓÇ£Compare battery drain causes from manual table + this photo of my usage screen.ΓÇ¥

Joint: First retrieve with hybrid (text keywords ΓÇ£battery drainΓÇ¥ + image embedding of usage screenshot) ΓåÆ top-50 candidates ΓåÆ cross-modal reranker (VLM scores how well each supports comparison) ΓåÆ pack top-8 ΓåÆ generate grounded answer with citations like ΓÇ£As shown in retrieved Table 1 (page 4) and your screenshot (Image 2) ΓÇªΓÇ¥.

Reranking for MRAG

Reranking is almost mandatory in MRAG because first-stage retrieval (bi-encoders) is recall-oriented but low precision.

Cross-modal reranker:

  • Input: query (text + optional image) + candidate (image/text/table/video snippet).
  • Output: relevance score (0ΓÇô1).
  • Models: fine-tuned FLMR, MM-R5 (reasoning-enhanced), or even LLaVA-Next as zero-shot reranker (ΓÇ£Rate how relevant this image/table is to the question on a scale 1ΓÇô10ΓÇ¥).

Answer-aware reranking:

  • Generate quick provisional answer from top-k raw retrieval.
  • Rerank candidates by how much they support/corroborate that provisional answer (e.g., entailment score or VLM reasoning trace).
  • Reduces modality bias (e.g., over-trusting noisy OCR text).

Iterative Retrieval (Multi-hop MRAG)

For complex questions (ΓÇ£Why does this smartwatch overheat during GPS? Check manual + reviews + similar user photos.ΓÇ¥), single-shot retrieval fails.

How iterative / multi-hop works:

  • Start with initial query ΓåÆ retrieve round 1 ΓåÆ VLM reasons (ΓÇ£Need GPS specs from manual + overheating images from reviewsΓÇ¥) ΓåÆ rewrite query multimodally (text rewrite + generated image query embedding) ΓåÆ retrieve round 2 ΓåÆ repeat 2ΓÇô4 times.
  • ΓÇ£Retrieve ΓåÆ reason ΓåÆ retrieve againΓÇ¥ loop (agentic style).
  • Query rewriting can be cross-modal: VLM generates ΓÇ£search for smartwatch with red overheating warning iconΓÇ¥ from initial text.

Initial query: ΓÇ£Is this laptop hinge issue common?ΓÇ¥ + photo.

Round 1: Retrieve similar hinge damage photos.

VLM reasons: ΓÇ£Need to check build quality in manual + user reviews.ΓÇ¥

Rewritten queries: text ΓÇ£laptop hinge durabilityΓÇ¥ + image embedding of hinge area.

Round 2: Retrieve manual page region + review snippets ΓåÆ final grounded answer.

Document & Table MRAG

PDFs

Born-digital (vector PDFs from manufacturer): text is extractable, layout preserved ΓåÆ use LayoutLMv3 or ColPali (image-based but layout-aware).

Scanned (customer invoices, old manuals): noisy OCR ΓåÆ ColPali shines because it embeds the rendered page image directly (no OCR needed) ΓåÆ much higher recall on tables/figures.

Layout sensitivity: ignore layout ΓåÆ table rows get scrambled. Preserve it ΓåÆ ColPali or DocLayout-YOLO keeps spatial meaning.

Table Handling

  • Table as text (classic): extract markdown ΓåÆ embed text ΓåÆ loses alignment/colors.
  • Table as image (vision-first): render table region ΓåÆ embed with ColPali ΓåÆ preserves visual structure.
  • Structure graph / dual-mode decomposition (state-of-the-art): parse table into graph (rows/columns/cells as nodes, relations as edges) + embed both text version and image version ΓåÆ retrieve jointly. Dual-mode = text path + vision path fused.

Visual Grounding in Documents

  • Page-region retrieval: ColPali retrieves whole pages but scores per patch ΓåÆ highlight relevant region (bounding box) in answer.
  • Cite snippets: Answer says ΓÇ£See Table 3, row 2, column ΓÇÿBattery LifeΓÇÖ on page 8 of retrieved PDF.ΓÇ¥
  • Traceability: Metadata + lineage (from earlier module) lets user click ΓÇ£why this page?ΓÇ¥ ΓåÆ shows retrieval score + region heat-map.

Query: ΓÇ£WhatΓÇÖs the IP rating difference between these two earbuds models?ΓÇ¥ + photo of packaging.

System: ColPali retrieves spec-sheet pages ΓåÆ dual-mode table decomposition pulls exact rows ΓåÆ visual grounding highlights IP68 vs IPX4 cells ΓåÆ answer ΓÇ£Model A has IP68 (dust & water resistant) vs Model BΓÇÖs IPX4 (splash only) ΓÇö see highlighted region in retrieved page image.ΓÇ¥

Context Packing: Formatting Mixed Content

  • Image placeholders + descriptions ("Image 1: cracked charging port").
  • Multimodal tokens (<image>, base64 if supported).
  • Tables ΓåÆ markdown + image.
  • Compression: key frames + highlights.
  • Grounding: "Only use visible info"; post-check claims.

Pitfall: Unordered 10 images ΓåÆ ignored. Solution: Relevance-sorted + labeled top 4ΓÇô6.

Conclusion & Key Takeaways

MRAG powers truly visual, grounded support, answering "Is this crack covered?" by seeing the photo, reading the policy, and citing both.

Key Takeaways

  • MRAG pipeline: Multimodal query ΓåÆ hybrid/joint retrieval ΓåÆ cross-modal rerank ΓåÆ careful packing ΓåÆ VLM generation with grounding.
  • Paradigms: Text-first (fast but lossy), vision-first (nuance but weak semantics), joint (best balance).
  • Reranking & iterative retrieval fix precision/hallucinations; ColPali excels on docs/tables.
  • Grounding + citations (regions, images) build trust ΓÇö critical for e-commerce returns/warranty.
  • In TechVista: Customer uploads defect photo ΓåÆ system sees it, retrieves policy regions/videos, generates cited, accurate response ΓåÆ fewer disputes, happier users.

Master MRAG and your system doesn't just read ΓÇö it sees, understands, and explains what it sees reliably

Quizzes

Multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation (MRAG)

Easy Questions

  1. What is the main difference between classic text RAG and MRAG?
    A) MRAG works only with text while classic RAG uses images
    B) MRAG handles queries and knowledge across text, images, tables, documents, video, and audio
    C) MRAG removes the need for any retrieval
    D) MRAG is slower and less powerful than text RAG
  2. What are the five core stages in a modern MRAG pipeline?
    A) Query understanding ΓåÆ retrieval ΓåÆ reranking ΓåÆ context packing ΓåÆ grounded generation
    B) Only retrieval and generation
    C) Only text encoding and decoding
    D) Only image captioning
  3. Which of the following is a valid retrieval target in MRAG?
    A) Only plain text chunks
    B) Image-text pairs, document regions with layout, video clips + transcripts
    C) Only audio files
    D) Only keywords
  4. What does ΓÇ£visual groundingΓÇ¥ mean in MRAG answers?
    A) The model cites the exact retrieved image or table region in its response
    B) The model ignores images completely
    C) The model only uses text
    D) The model generates new images

Medium Questions

  1. What are the three main retrieval paradigms in MRAG?
    A) Text-first, vision-first, and joint retrieval
    B) Only text-first and image-only
    C) Only hybrid and late fusion
    D) Only early fusion and cross-attention
  2. Why is reranking almost mandatory in MRAG?
    A) First-stage retrieval (bi-encoders) is recall-oriented but low precision; reranking improves relevance using cross-modal or answer-aware scorers
    B) It makes retrieval slower
    C) It removes the need for any retrieval
    D) It only works for text
  3. What is a common practical technique for handling tables and documents in MRAG?
    A) Use ColPali (image-based, layout-aware embedding) or dual-mode decomposition (text + vision path)
    B) Convert everything to plain text only
    C) Ignore layout completely
    D) Use only OCR without vision

Hard Questions

  1. In iterative/multi-hop MRAG, what happens after the first retrieval round?
    A) The VLM reasons over the results, rewrites the query multimodally, and triggers additional retrieval rounds until the answer is sufficiently grounded
    B) The process stops immediately
    C) Only one retrieval is ever done
    D) The model ignores previous results
  2. What is one major pitfall of naive context packing in MRAG?
    A) Feeding too many unordered images/tables causes the VLM to ignore parts of the context or hallucinate
    B) It always improves accuracy
    C) It reduces token usage
    D) It works perfectly with any number of images
  3. According to the document, why is MRAG especially valuable for e-commerce support (e.g., TechVista warranty claims)?
    A) The system can ΓÇ£seeΓÇ¥ the customer-uploaded damage photo, retrieve relevant policy regions/tables/videos, and generate a cited, accurate response
    B) It only uses text descriptions
    C) It increases hallucinations
    D) It cannot handle photos

Answers for Multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation (MRAG)

  1. B
  2. A
  3. B
  4. A
  5. A
  6. A
  7. A
  8. A
  9. A
  10. A
